answer: Section 138 IPC provides that whoever abets what he knows to be an act of insubordination by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman, in the Army, Navy or Air Force, of the Government of India, shall, if such act of insubordination be committed in consequence of that abetment, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 6 months, or with fine, or with both.

question: What is wearing garb or carrying token used by soldier with intent to deceive?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 140 IPC deals with impersonating military personnel.

Step 2: Establish false appearance - Wearing military uniform or carrying military token.

Step 3: Define intent - To deceive or make people believe person is in military service.

Step 4: Note fraud on public - Exploits respect for military to gain advantage.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 3 months or fine up to Rs. 500 or both; prevents military impersonation.
answer: Section 140 IPC provides that whoever, not being a soldier, sailor or airman in the Military, Naval or Air service of the Government of India, wears any garb or carries any token resembling any garb or token used by such a soldier, sailor or airman with the intention that it may be believed that he is such a soldier, sailor or airman, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 months, or with fine which may extend to Rs. 500, or with both.

question: What is unlawful assembly?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 141 IPC defines unlawful assembly.

Step 2: Establish numerical requirement - Five or more persons assembled.

Step 3: Define five common objects - (1) Resist law execution, (2) Commit offense, (3) Use criminal force against officials, (4) Take property by force, (5) Compel illegal acts by force.

Step 4: Note collective criminality - Group crime more dangerous than individual acts.

Step 5: Understand punishment (Section 143) - Imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both for membership; higher if assembly armed or riots.
answer: Section 141 IPC defines unlawful assembly as an assembly of five or more persons having common object to: (1) overawe government or legislature, (2) resist execution of law, (3) commit mischief or criminal trespass, (4) obtain possession of property by force, or (5) compel any person to do illegal act. Section 143 punishes membership with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.

question: What is joining or continuing in unlawful assembly knowing it has been commanded to disperse?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 145 IPC deals with defying dispersal order.

Step 2: Establish knowledge of command - Knowing that lawful command to disperse has been given.

Step 3: Define continuing - Joining or continuing in assembly after dispersal order.

Step 4: Note defiance of authority - Refusing lawful order shows increased culpability.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both; same as basic membership but reflects defiance.
answer: Section 145 IPC provides that whoever joins or continues in an unlawful assembly, knowing that such unlawful assembly has been commanded in the manner prescribed by law to disperse, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 6 months, or with fine, or with both. This punishes defiance of dispersal orders.

question: What is rioting?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 146 IPC defines rioting.

Step 2: Establish force/violence - Unlawful assembly using force or violence.

Step 3: Define collective violence - Members use force in prosecution of common object.

Step 4: Note escalation - Rioting is unlawful assembly that turns violent.

Step 5: Understand punishment (Section 147) - Imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both; more than unlawful assembly (6 months) due to violence.
answer: Section 146 IPC defines rioting as whenever force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly, or by any member thereof, in prosecution of the common object of such assembly, every member of such assembly is guilty of the offense of rioting. Section 147 punishes rioting with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.

question: What is every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offense committed in prosecution of common object?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 149 IPC deals with constructive liability of assembly members.

Step 2: Establish joint liability - Every member guilty of offense committed in furtherance of common object.

Step 3: Define scope - Includes offenses members knew likely to be committed.

Step 4: Note collective responsibility - All members liable for acts of any member.

Step 5: Understand application - If murder committed during riot, all rioters can be charged with murder if it was in furtherance of common object.
answer: Section 149 IPC provides that if an offense is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or such as the members of that assembly knew to be likely to be committed, every person who was a member of the assembly at the time of committing of that offense is guilty of that offense. This establishes constructive liability for all members.

question: What is promoting enmity between different groups?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 153A IPC deals with promoting enmity.

Step 2: Establish protected grounds - Religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, caste, community.

Step 3: Define prohibited acts - Promoting disharmony, enmity, hatred, or ill-will by words, signs, representations.

Step 4: Note communal harmony goal - Prevents incitement that can lead to violence.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both; if in place of worship: up to 5 years and fine.
answer: Section 153A IPC provides that whoever by words, signs, visible representations, promotes or attempts to promote disharmony or feelings of enmity, hatred or ill-will between different religious, racial, language or regional groups or castes or communities, shall be punished with imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both. If committed in place of worship or religious assembly: up to 5 years and fine.

question: What is assertions prejudicial to national integration?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 153B IPC deals with assertions prejudicial to integration.

Step 2: Establish national unity context - Assertions that members of group cannot bear true faith to Constitution.

Step 3: Define prohibited imputations - Suggesting group disloyal to nation based on religion, race, language, caste, etc.

Step 4: Note sovereignty protection - Prevents questioning loyalty of communities.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both; if in place of worship: up to 5 years and fine.
answer: Section 153B IPC provides that whoever makes or publishes any imputation that any class of persons cannot bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution of India or uphold the sovereignty and integrity of India by reason of being members of any religious, racial, language or regional group, shall be punished with imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.

question: What is causing fear or alarm to public?
reasoning:
Step 1: This relates to various provisions including Section 153A, 153B, and public nuisance provisions.

Step 2: Establish alarm element - Acts causing fear or alarm to members of public.

Step 3: Define intent - Intentionally or knowingly causing alarm or fear.

Step 4: Note public order concern - Mass panic can lead to violence and disorder.

Step 5: Understand multiple provisions apply - Specific sections depend on nature of act (communal, terrorist, etc.).
answer: Causing fear or alarm to public is addressed through various IPC provisions depending on the means: Section 153A (promoting enmity), Section 505 (statements conducing to public mischief), Section 506 (criminal intimidation), and Section 507 (criminal intimidation by anonymous communication). Punishment ranges from 2 to 7 years depending on the specific provision and circumstances.

question: What is defiling place of worship with intent to insult religion?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 295 IPC deals with defiling places of worship.

Step 2: Establish sacred place - Place of worship or sacred object.

Step 3: Define defiling - Destroying, damaging, or defiling with intent to insult religion.

Step 4: Note religious sentiments - Protects sanctity of religious places and objects.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both; maintains religious harmony.
answer: Section 295 IPC provides that whoever destroys, damages or defiles any place of worship, or any object held sacred by any class of persons with the intention of thereby insulting the religion of any class of persons or with the knowledge that any class of persons is likely to consider such destruction, damage or defilement as an insult to their religion, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, or with fine, or with both.

question: What is disturbing religious assembly?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 296 IPC deals with disturbing religious assemblies.

Step 2: Establish assembly context - Religious assembly lawfully engaged in religious worship or ceremonies.

Step 3: Define disturbance - Voluntarily causing disturbance to assembly.

Step 4: Note freedom of religion - Protects right to peacefully practice religion.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both; ensures peaceful worship.
answer: Section 296 IPC provides that whoever voluntarily causes disturbance to any assembly lawfully engaged in the performance of religious worship, or religious ceremonies, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 1 year, or with fine, or with both.

question: What is trespassing on burial places?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 297 IPC deals with trespass on burial grounds.

Step 2: Establish protected places - Burial place, sepulcher, or place set apart for funeral rites.

Step 3: Define trespass - With intent to wound feelings or insult religion, or knowing likely to do so.

Step 4: Note respect for dead - Protects sanctity of burial places and funeral customs.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both; respects burial traditions.
answer: Section 297 IPC provides that whoever, with the intention of wounding the feelings of any person, or of insulting the religion of any person, or with the knowledge that the feelings of any person are likely to be wounded, or that the religion of any person is likely to be insulted thereby, commits any trespass in any place of worship or on any place of sepulture, or any place set apart for the performance of funeral rites or as a depository for the remains of the dead, shall be punished with imprisonment up to 1 year, or with fine, or with both.

question: What is uttering words with deliberate intent to wound religious feelings?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 298 IPC deals with wounding religious feelings.

Step 2: Establish utterance - Words, sounds, gestures, or visible representations.

Step 3: Define intent - Deliberate intention to wound religious feelings of any person.

Step 4: Note must be heard/seen - Utterance must be within hearing or sight of person whose feelings intended to wound.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both; less than Section 295A which requires malicious intent to outrage religious feelings of class.
answer: Section 298 IPC provides that whoever, with the deliberate intention of wounding the religious feelings of any person, utters any word or makes any sound in the hearing of that person or makes any gesture in the sight of that person or places any object in the sight of that person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 1 year, or with fine, or with both.

question: What is sale of obscene books?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 292 IPC deals with sale of obscene material.

Step 2: Establish obscene material - Books, pamphlets, papers, drawings, paintings, representations, figures that are obscene.

Step 3: Define prohibited acts - Selling, letting to hire, distributing, publicly exhibiting, or circulating.

Step 4: Note public morality - Protects society from obscene material.

Step 5: Understand punishment - First conviction: imprisonment up to 2 years and fine up to Rs. 2000; subsequent conviction: imprisonment up to 5 years and fine up to Rs. 5000.
answer: Section 292 IPC provides that whoever sells, lets to hire, distributes, publicly exhibits or in any manner puts into circulation any obscene book, pamphlet, paper, drawing, painting, representation or figure shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment up to 2 years and fine up to Rs. 2000, and on second or subsequent conviction with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine up to Rs. 5000.

question: What is sale of obscene objects to young person?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 293 IPC deals with sale of obscene material to minors.

Step 2: Establish age protection - Person under twenty years of age.

Step 3: Define prohibited conduct - Selling, letting to hire, distributing, or showing obscene objects to young person.

Step 4: Note enhanced protection for minors - Special protection for youth from obscene material.

Step 5: Understand punishment - First conviction: imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to Rs. 2000; subsequent conviction: imprisonment up to 7 years and fine up to Rs. 5000.
answer: Section 293 IPC provides that whoever sells, lets to hire, distributes, exhibits or circulates to any person under the age of twenty years any such obscene object as is referred to in Section 292, shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to Rs. 2000, and on second or subsequent conviction with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine up to Rs. 5000.

question: What is obscene acts and songs?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 294 IPC deals with obscene acts in public.

Step 2: Establish public context - Act done in any public place.

Step 3: Define obscene acts - Any obscene act, or singing, reciting, or uttering obscene songs, ballads, or words.

Step 4: Note annoyance requirement - To annoyance of others.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 3 months or fine or both; maintains public decency.
answer: Section 294 IPC provides that whoever, to the annoyance of others, does any obscene act in any public place, or sings, recites or utters any obscene song, ballad or words, in or near any public place, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 months, or with fine, or with both.

question: What is keeping lottery office?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 294A IPC deals with keeping lottery offices.

Step 2: Establish prohibited activity - Keeping office or place for purpose of drawing lottery not authorized by state government.

Step 3: Define scope - Publishing proposals relating to lottery.

Step 4: Note gambling regulation - States regulate lotteries; unauthorized lotteries are illegal.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both; regulates gambling activities.
answer: Section 294A IPC provides that whoever keeps any office or place for the purpose of drawing any lottery not being a State lottery or a lottery authorised by the State Government, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 6 months, or with fine, or with both. And whoever publishes any proposal to pay any sum on any event or contingency relative or applicable to the drawing of any ticket in such lottery shall be punished with fine up to Rs. 1000.

question: What is injury?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 44 IPC defines injury.

Step 2: Establish broad definition - Any harm whatever illegally caused to any person.

Step 3: Define scope - In body, mind, reputation, or property.

Step 4: Note comprehensiveness - Covers all forms of harm, not just physical.

Step 5: Understand application - This definition applies throughout IPC wherever 'injury' is mentioned.
answer: Section 44 IPC defines injury as any harm whatever illegally caused to any person, in body, mind, reputation or property. This is a comprehensive definition that encompasses physical hurt, mental anguish, damage to reputation, and harm to property. It is used throughout the IPC to establish elements of various offenses.

question: What is dishonestly?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 24 IPC defines dishonestly.

Step 2: Establish wrongful intent - Doing anything with intention of causing wrongful gain or wrongful loss.

Step 3: Define key terms - Wrongful gain: gain by unlawful means; wrongful loss: loss by unlawful means.

Step 4: Note mens rea element - Dishonesty is state of mind, not just act itself.

Step 5: Understand application - Essential element in theft, cheating, criminal breach of trust, etc.
answer: Section 24 IPC defines that whoever does anything with the intention of causing wrongful gain to one person or wrongful loss to another person, is said to do that thing 'dishonestly'. Wrongful gain is gain by unlawful means of property to which person gaining is not legally entitled; wrongful loss is loss by unlawful means of property to which person losing is legally entitled.

question: What is fraudulently?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 25 IPC defines fraudulently.

Step 2: Establish deception element - Doing something with intent to defraud.

Step 3: Define defraud - Not explicitly defined but implies deceit or trickery.

Step 4: Note distinction from dishonestly - Fraudulently implies deception; dishonestly can exist without deception.

Step 5: Understand application - Used in offenses like cheating, fraudulent execution of deeds, etc.
answer: Section 25 IPC provides that a person is said to do a thing fraudulently if he does that thing with intent to defraud but not otherwise. While not explicitly defining 'defraud', it implies use of deceit or trickery to cause wrongful gain or loss. Fraudulently is distinct from dishonestly in that it necessarily involves an element of deception.

question: What is reason to believe?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 26 IPC defines reason to believe.

Step 2: Establish knowledge standard - Person has sufficient cause to believe, not actual knowledge.

Step 3: Define objective test - What reasonable person in same circumstances would believe.

Step 4: Note lower than actual knowledge - Don't need proof person actually knew; sufficient if should have known.

Step 5: Understand application - Used in receiving stolen property, harbouring offenders, etc.
answer: Section 26 IPC provides that a person is said to have 'reason to believe' a thing, if he has sufficient cause to believe that thing but not otherwise. This is an objective standard based on what a reasonable person in the same circumstances would believe, and is lower than actual knowledge but higher than mere suspicion.

question: What is property?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 22 IPC defines property.

Step 2: Establish broad scope - Includes movable property, immovable property, and property of every description.

Step 3: Define extent - Whether corporeal or incorporeal.

Step 4: Note comprehensiveness - Very broad definition to cover all forms of property.

Step 5: Understand application - Property offenses (theft, robbery, extortion, etc.) apply to all these forms.
answer: Section 22 IPC defines property as including property of every description, whether corporeal or incorporeal, movable or immovable, and includes any right or interest in such property. This comprehensive definition ensures all forms of property are protected under property offense provisions of IPC.

question: What is movable property?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 22 IPC addresses movable property as part of property definition.

Step 2: Establish movability - Property capable of being moved from one place to another.

Step 3: Define examples - Money, goods, chattels, crops, standing timber.

Step 4: Note significance - Theft primarily concerns movable property.

Step 5: Understand distinction - Immovable property (land, buildings) cannot be stolen but can be subject to other offenses.
answer: Movable property under Section 22 IPC includes property of every description except land and things attached to the earth or permanently fastened to anything attached to the earth. This includes corporeal movables (money, goods, chattels) and can include standing timber, growing crops and grass. Theft, robbery, and extortion primarily concern movable property.

question: What is valuable security?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 30 IPC defines valuable security.

Step 2: Establish document requirement - Any document which is or purports to be creating, transferring, or extinguishing legal rights.

Step 3: Define scope - Includes documents creating right to property, marks of value, etc.

Step 4: Note broad application - Checks, bonds, promissory notes, share certificates all qualify.

Step 5: Understand significance - Stealing or extorting valuable securities is as serious as stealing actual property.
answer: Section 30 IPC defines valuable security as a document which is, or purports to be, a document whereby any legal right is created, extended, transferred, restricted, extinguished or released, or whereby any person acknowledges that he lies under legal liability, or has not a certain legal right. This includes documents like promissory notes, bonds, and receipts that have monetary or legal value.

question: What is a will?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 31 IPC defines will for purposes of IPC.

Step 2: Establish testamentary nature - Document expressing testamentary intention.

Step 3: Define scope - Includes codicil and testamentary document of any description.

Step 4: Note protection - Forging, destroying, or stealing wills are specific offenses.

Step 5: Understand significance - Wills determine property distribution after death; tampering with them is serious.
answer: Section 31 IPC provides that the word 'will' denotes any testamentary document. This broad definition includes formal wills, codicils, and any other document expressing testamentary intentions regarding disposition of property after death. Offenses relating to wills (forgery, destruction, theft) are provided for in various IPC sections.

question: What is words referring to acts include illegal omissions?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 32 IPC extends act definitions.

Step 2: Establish inclusion - References to 'act' include illegal omissions.

Step 3: Define omission - Failure to do something one is legally bound to do.

Step 4: Note broadening of liability - Can be criminally liable for not acting when duty to act exists.

Step 5: Understand application - Person can commit murder by omission (e.g., parent starving child).
answer: Section 32 IPC provides that in every part of the Code, except where a contrary intention appears from the context, words which refer to acts done extend also to illegal omissions. This means criminal liability can arise from failure to perform legal duties. For example, a parent can commit culpable homicide by omitting to feed their child.

question: What is act and omission?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 33 IPC defines act and omission.

Step 2: Establish single act - Series of acts or omissions can form one offense.

Step 3: Define combined liability - Offense can be constituted by series of acts/omissions.

Step 4: Note practical application - Theft by taking multiple items over time still one offense of theft.

Step 5: Understand significance - Prevents loophole where person claims each act was too minor to be offense.
answer: Section 33 IPC provides that the word 'act' denotes as well a series of acts as a single act, and the word 'omission' denotes as well a series of omissions as a single omission. This ensures that an offense can be constituted by a series of smaller acts or omissions taken together, preventing claims that individual acts were too minor to constitute an offense.

question: What is voluntarily?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 39 IPC defines voluntarily.

Step 2: Establish intent/knowledge requirement - Person causes effect voluntarily if intends or knows likely to cause it.

Step 3: Define scope - Effect caused by means of which effect is intended or known to be likely.

Step 4: Note mens rea - Voluntariness is key element distinguishing criminal from accidental acts.

Step 5: Understand application - 'Voluntarily causes hurt' means intends or knows likely to cause hurt.
answer: Section 39 IPC provides that a person is said to cause an effect 'voluntarily' when he causes it by means whereby he intended to cause it, or by means which, at the time of employing those means, he knew or had reason to believe to be likely to cause it. This establishes that voluntary acts require either intention or knowledge of likely consequences.

question: What is offense?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 40 IPC defines offense.

Step 2: Establish legal violation - Thing made punishable under IPC or any special or local law.

Step 3: Define scope - Not limited to IPC; includes all penal legislation.

Step 4: Note comprehensiveness - Ensures IPC provisions apply to offenses under other laws too.

Step 5: Understand significance - Abetment, attempt, conspiracy provisions apply to offenses under any law.
answer: Section 40 IPC provides that the word 'offense' denotes a thing made punishable by the IPC, or by any special or local law for the time being in force. This broad definition ensures that general provisions of IPC (like those on abetment, attempt, and criminal conspiracy) apply not just to IPC offenses but also to offenses under special and local laws.

question: What is special law?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 41 IPC defines special law.

Step 2: Establish applicability - Law applicable to particular subject.

Step 3: Define scope - Laws other than IPC that deal with specific matters.

Step 4: Note examples - NDPS Act, Prevention of Corruption Act, IT Act, etc.

Step 5: Understand relationship - IPC is general law; special laws override IPC when conflict exists.
answer: Section 41 IPC provides that 'special law' means a law applicable to a particular subject. Special laws are distinct from the IPC which is a general penal law. Examples include the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act, Prevention of Corruption Act, and Information Technology Act. When special law and IPC conflict, special law generally prevails.

question: What is local law?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 42 IPC defines local law.

Step 2: Establish territorial limitation - Law applicable only to particular part of India.

Step 3: Define scope - State-specific legislation, municipal laws, etc.

Step 4: Note federal structure - India's federal system allows states to make local laws.

Step 5: Understand application - IPC provisions on abetment, etc. apply to local law offenses too.
answer: Section 42 IPC provides that 'local law' means a law applicable only to a particular part of India. This recognizes that in addition to central laws like IPC, states and local authorities can enact laws applicable only within their jurisdictions. General IPC provisions apply to offenses under local laws unless the local law provides otherwise.

question: What is illegal and legally bound to do?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 43 IPC defines illegal and legally bound.

Step 2: Establish illegality - Everything which is an offense or prohibited by law or furnishing ground for civil action.

Step 3: Define legal binding - Person legally bound to do something if legal consequence follows from not doing it.

Step 4: Note broad scope - Illegal includes criminal offenses, civil wrongs, and regulatory violations.

Step 5: Understand application - Used in provisions like 'compel to do illegal act' or 'legally bound to give information'.
answer: Section 43 IPC provides that the word 'illegal' is applicable to everything which is an offense or which is prohibited by law, or which furnishes ground for a civil action. A person is said to be 'legally bound to do' a thing if by law requires that person to do that thing, even if not legally enforceable by physical compulsion. These definitions establish standards for lawful conduct throughout IPC.

question: What is good faith?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 52 IPC defines good faith.

Step 2: Establish subjective element - Nothing is said to be done or believed in good faith if done/believed without due care and attention.

Step 3: Define standard - Requires both honest belief and reasonable care.

Step 4: Note defense - Good faith is defense to many offenses (e.g., acts of public servants).

Step 5: Understand application - Not enough to act honestly; must also act carefully and reasonably.
answer: Section 52 IPC provides that nothing is said to be done or believed in 'good faith' which is done or believed without due care and attention. Good faith requires both subjective honesty and objective reasonableness. It is an important defense in many IPC provisions, particularly those involving acts of public servants or causing miscarriage to save woman's life.

question: What is harbour?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 52A IPC defines harbour.

Step 2: Establish elements - Supplying person with shelter, food, drink, money, clothes, arms, ammunition, or means of conveyance.

Step 3: Define assistance - Help offender evade apprehension.

Step 4: Note active conduct - Harbouring is active assistance, not mere failure to report.

Step 5: Understand application - Used in harbouring offenders, deserters, prisoners provisions.
answer: Section 52A IPC provides that a person is said to 'harbour' another person when he has knowingly procured, or knowingly attempted to procure, shelter, food, drink, money, clothes, arms, ammunition or means of conveyance for that other person, or has knowingly assisted him in evading apprehension. This defines the active assistance required for harbouring offenses.

question: What is document?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 29 IPC defines document.

Step 2: Establish broad scope - Any matter expressed or described upon any substance by marks or other means.

Step 3: Define recording - Intended to be used or which may be used for recording that matter.

Step 4: Note modern interpretation - Includes electronic documents, though Electronic Records are separately covered under IT Act.

Step 5: Understand application - Forgery, using forged documents, destruction of documents all require this definition.
answer: Section 29 IPC defines 'document' as any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, as evidence of that matter. This includes maps, plans, drawings, photographs, and in modern interpretation, electronic documents.

question: What is public servant?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 21 IPC defines public servant extensively.

Step 2: Establish categories - Lists 12 categories including government officers, judges, police, military, elected representatives.

Step 3: Define scope - Every person in service of government or holding office by authority of government.

Step 4: Note purpose - Special provisions for offenses by/against public servants.

Step 5: Understand breadth - Very broad definition to ensure public accountability.
answer: Section 21 IPC provides an extensive definition of 'public servant' including: every commissioned officer in military/naval/air force; every judge; persons empowered to perform judicial acts; officers of court of justice; government officers; police officers; officers with authority to take evidence; persons holding any office by government authority; and any person instructed or commanded by public servant to assist. This comprehensive definition ensures accountability of all government functionaries.

question: What is court of justice?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 20 IPC defines Court of Justice.

Step 2: Establish judicial authority - Judge appointed by government or authorized to act as such.

Step 3: Define judicial function - Person empowered by law to give definitive judgment or final judgment under conditions.

Step 4: Note purpose - Protects judicial process and establishes jurisdiction for perjury, contempt, etc.

Step 5: Understand scope - Includes all courts and tribunals with judicial powers.
answer: Section 20 IPC provides that 'Court of Justice' means a Judge who is empowered by law to act judicially alone, or a body of Judges which is empowered by law to act judicially as a body. This includes all courts from Supreme Court to Magistrate courts, and tribunals empowered to give definitive or final judgments on matters brought before them.

question: What is causing a thing to be done?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 36 IPC addresses effect caused partly by act and partly by omission.

Step 2: Establish combined causation - Effect can be caused by combination of acts and omissions.

Step 3: Define liability - Person liable if any part of act or omission is criminal.

Step 4: Note practical significance - Can't escape liability by claiming only part of conduct was wrongful.

Step 5: Understand application - If person does act knowing omission will cause harmful effect, liable for both.
answer: Section 36 IPC provides that wherever the causing of a certain effect, or an attempt to cause that effect, by an act or by an omission, is an offense, it is to be understood that the causing of that effect partly by an act and partly by an omission is the same offense. This ensures liability where harmful result is caused by combination of acts and omissions.

question: What is co-operation by doing one of several acts constituting an offense?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 37 IPC addresses cooperation in committing offenses.

Step 2: Establish multiple participants - When offense requires combination of several acts done by different persons.

Step 3: Define cooperation - Each person doing part of the acts necessary to complete offense.

Step 4: Note joint liability - All participants liable even though each did only part.

Step 5: Understand punishment - If A holds victim while B strikes, both guilty of assault; cooperation makes both liable for entire offense.
answer: Section 37 IPC provides that when an offense is committed by means of several acts, whoever intentionally co-operates in the commission of that offense by doing any one of those acts, either singly or jointly with any other person, commits that offense. This establishes that participation in part of a multi-act offense makes one liable for the entire offense.

question: What is criminal breach of trust?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 405 IPC defines criminal breach of trust.

Step 2: Establish entrustment - Property or dominion over property entrusted to person.

Step 3: Define breach - Dishonestly misappropriating, converting to own use, or using/disposing in violation of law or legal contract.

Step 4: Note trust violation - Betrayal of confidence reposed in trustee.

Step 5: Understand punishment (Section 406) - Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both; by carrier, clerk, servant: up to 7 years (Section 408).
answer: Section 405 IPC defines criminal breach of trust as whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law or of any legal contract, commits criminal breach of trust. Section 406 punishes it with imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.

question: What is criminal breach of trust by carrier?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 407 IPC deals with breach of trust by carriers.

Step 2: Establish carrier relationship - Carrier, wharfinger, or warehouse-keeper entrusted with property.

Step 3: Define commercial trust - Special relationship in commerce and transport.

Step 4: Note enhanced culpability - Breach undermines commercial transportation system.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine; enhanced from basic breach of trust (3 years) due to commercial importance.
answer: Section 407 IPC provides that whoever, being entrusted with property as a carrier, wharfinger or warehouse-keeper, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of such property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is criminal breach of trust by clerk or servant?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 408 IPC deals with breach of trust by employees.

Step 2: Establish employment relationship - Clerk, servant, or employee.

Step 3: Define workplace betrayal - Employee misappropriating employer's property.

Step 4: Note abuse of position - Violates trust essential to employment.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine; reflects serious breach of employment trust.
answer: Section 408 IPC provides that whoever, being a clerk or servant or employed as a clerk or servant, and being in any manner entrusted in such capacity with property or with any dominion over property, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of that property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is criminal breach of trust by public servant, banker, merchant or agent?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 409 IPC deals with breach of trust by fiduciaries.

Step 2: Establish positions of high trust - Public servants, bankers, merchants, agents.

Step 3: Define grave breach - Persons in positions of public or commercial trust.

Step 4: Note maximum culpability - Highest positions of trust receive strictest punishment.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment for life or up to 10 years and fine; most severe punishment for breach of trust.
answer: Section 409 IPC provides that whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property in his capacity as a public servant or in the way of his business as a banker, merchant, factor, broker, attorney or agent, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of that property, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is stolen property?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 410 IPC defines stolen property.

Step 2: Establish acquisition by offense - Property obtained through theft, extortion, robbery, criminal misappropriation, or breach of trust.

Step 3: Define scope - Includes property acquired by any of five property offenses.

Step 4: Note chain of possession - Property remains stolen even if passes through multiple hands.

Step 5: Understand significance - Determines applicability of 'receiving stolen property' offenses.
answer: Section 410 IPC provides that property, the possession whereof has been transferred by theft, or by extortion, or by robbery, and property which has been criminally misappropriated or in respect of which criminal breach of trust has been committed, is designated as 'stolen property'. This remains stolen property whether in possession of thief or any other person.

question: What is dishonestly receiving stolen property?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 411 IPC deals with receiving stolen property.

Step 2: Establish knowledge requirement - Knowing or having reason to believe property is stolen.

Step 3: Define dishonest receipt - Receiving or retaining stolen goods dishonestly.

Step 4: Note market creation - Creates demand for stolen goods, encouraging theft.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both; enhanced if property stolen in dacoity: life or 10 years (Section 412).
answer: Section 411 IPC provides that whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine, or with both.

question: What is dishonestly receiving property stolen in dacoity?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 412 IPC deals with receiving dacoity proceeds.

Step 2: Establish violent origin - Property stolen through dacoity (violent group robbery).

Step 3: Define aggravating factor - Receiving proceeds of violent organized crime.

Step 4: Note encouragement of violence - Creates market for violently obtained goods.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine; reflects gravity of dacoity.
answer: Section 412 IPC provides that whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, the possession whereof he knows or has reason to believe to have been transferred by the commission of dacoity, or dishonestly receives from a person whom he knows or has reason to believe to belong or to have belonged to a gang of dacoits, property which he knows or has reason to believe to have been stolen, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is habitually dealing in stolen property?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 414 IPC (second part) deals with habitual receivers.

Step 2: Establish pattern of conduct - Person habitually receives or deals in stolen property.

Step 3: Define organized receiving - Regular dealing suggests connection to theft networks.

Step 4: Note professional fence - Creates sustained market for stolen goods.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment for life or up to 10 years and fine; much higher than single receipt (3 years).
answer: Section 414 IPC (second part) provides that if a person habitually receives or deals in property which he knows or has reason to believe to be stolen property, he shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall also be liable to fine. This targets professional fences and organized receivers.

question: What is assisting in concealment or disposal of stolen property?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 414 IPC (first part) deals with assisting concealment.

Step 2: Establish voluntary assistance - Helping to conceal or dispose of stolen property.

Step 3: Define knowledge requirement - Knowing or having reason to believe property is stolen.

Step 4: Note facilitation - Helps thieves by hiding evidence and moving stolen goods.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both; facilitates theft by helping thieves.
answer: Section 414 IPC (first part) provides that whoever voluntarily assists in concealing or disposing of or making away with property which he knows or has reason to believe to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine, or with both.

question: What is cheating?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 415 IPC defines cheating.

Step 2: Establish deception - Deceiving person fraudulently or dishonestly.

Step 3: Define four modes - (1) Inducing to deliver property, (2) Inducing to consent to retain property, (3) Inducing to do/omit act causing harm, (4) Using dishonest concealment of facts.

Step 4: Note fraud element - Deception for gain or to cause loss.

Step 5: Understand punishment (Section 417) - Imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both; enhanced if by personation: up to 3 years (Section 419).
answer: Section 415 IPC defines cheating as whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to 'cheat'.

question: What is cheating by personation?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 416 IPC defines cheating by personation.

Step 2: Establish impersonation - Pretending to be another person or substituting one for another.

Step 3: Define false identity - Using false identity as means of cheating.

Step 4: Note identity fraud - Modern applications include online impersonation, phishing.

Step 5: Understand punishment (Section 419) - Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both; higher than basic cheating (1 year).
answer: Section 416 IPC defines cheating by personation as cheating by pretending to be some other person, or by knowingly substituting one person for another, or representing that he or any other person is a person other than he or such other person really is. Section 419 punishes it with imprisonment of either description up to 3 years, or with fine, or with both.

question: What is punishment for cheating?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 417 IPC punishes cheating.

Step 2: Establish basic offense - Cheating as defined in Section 415.

Step 3: Define relatively light punishment - Reflects property crime without violence.

Step 4: Note enhanced punishment for specific types - Cheating by personation (Section 419), cheating in relation to property delivery (Section 420).

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both for basic cheating.
answer: Section 417 IPC provides that whoever cheats shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 1 year, or with fine, or with both. However, cheating by personation (Section 419) carries up to 3 years, and cheating with dishonest inducement to deliver property (Section 420) carries up to 7 years.

question: What is cheating with knowledge that wrongful loss may ensue to person whose interest offender is bound to protect?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 418 IPC deals with breach of fiduciary duty through cheating.

Step 2: Establish fiduciary relationship - Offender bound to protect victim's interests.

Step 3: Define betrayal - Cheating person whose interests one is duty-bound to protect.

Step 4: Note dual wrong - Both cheating and breach of fiduciary duty.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both; reflects betrayal of trust.
answer: Section 418 IPC provides that whoever cheats with the knowledge that he is likely thereby to cause wrongful loss to a person whose interest in the transaction to which the cheating relates, he was bound, either by law, or by a legal contract, to protect, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine, or with both.

question: What is punishment for cheating by personation?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 419 IPC punishes cheating by personation.

Step 2: Establish aggravated form - Cheating combined with impersonation.

Step 3: Define identity fraud - Using false identity to defraud.

Step 4: Note increased culpability - Personation shows greater planning and deception.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both; triple the basic cheating (Section 417: 1 year).
answer: Section 419 IPC provides that whoever cheats by personation shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine, or with both. This enhanced punishment reflects the aggravated nature of using false identity to commit fraud.

question: What is cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 420 IPC deals with property fraud.

Step 2: Establish property element - Cheating that induces delivery of property or valuable security.

Step 3: Define dishonest inducement - Making or altering valuable security or causing property delivery.

Step 4: Note most common fraud - Covers typical fraud scenarios involving property transfer.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine; most severe cheating punishment reflecting property loss.
answer: Section 420 IPC provides that whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, or anything which is signed or sealed, and which is capable of being converted into a valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is house-trespass?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 442 IPC defines house-trespass.

Step 2: Establish unlawful entry - Criminal trespass in building, tent, or vessel used as dwelling or property custody.

Step 3: Define protected spaces - Human dwellings and property storage areas.

Step 4: Note privacy violation - Entry into private residential or storage spaces.

Step 5: Understand punishment (Section 448) - Imprisonment up to 1 year or fine up to Rs. 1000 or both; basic house-trespass offense.
answer: Section 442 IPC defines house-trespass as whoever commits criminal trespass by entering into or remaining in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or any building used as a place for worship, or as a place for the custody of property, is said to commit house-trespass. Section 448 punishes it with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine up to Rs. 1000 or both.

question: What is house-breaking?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 445 IPC defines house-breaking.

Step 2: Establish six modes of entry/exit - Passage made by breaking, climbing, using false keys, opening locked passage, using criminal force, or entering secretly.

Step 3: Define forcible/unauthorized entry - Entry not through normal authorized means.

Step 4: Note aggravated trespass - More serious than simple house-trespass due to manner of entry.

Step 5: Understand punishment varies - Depends on purpose and circumstances (Sections 449-460); can be up to life imprisonment.
answer: Section 445 IPC defines house-breaking as committing house-trespass if entry or exit is in any of six ways: (1) through passage made by breaking, (2) by climbing over, (3) through opening not intended for entry/exit, (4) by opening locked passage with false keys, (5) by using criminal force or committing assault, or (6) by entering/exiting in secret manner. Punishment varies from 2 years to life imprisonment depending on circumstances.

question: What is lurking house-trespass?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 443 IPC defines lurking house-trespass.

Step 2: Establish concealment element - House-trespass with precautions to conceal presence.

Step 3: Define stealth - Taking measures to avoid detection by occupant.

Step 4: Note aggravated nature - Concealment shows criminal intent and planning.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Enhanced compared to simple house-trespass; varies based on purpose and time of commission.
answer: Section 443 IPC defines lurking house-trespass as house-trespass committed after taking precautions to conceal such house-trespass from some person who has a right to exclude or eject the trespasser from the building, tent or vessel which is the subject of the trespass. The concealment element makes it more serious than simple house-trespass.

question: What is lurking house-trespass by night?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 444 IPC defines lurking house-trespass by night.

Step 2: Establish time element - Committed after sunset and before sunrise.

Step 3: Define maximum aggravation - Combines concealment with nighttime commission.

Step 4: Note enhanced danger - Occupants asleep, detection harder, greater fear.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Most severe among trespass offenses; up to 14 years if combined with preparation for hurt.
answer: Section 444 IPC defines lurking house-trespass by night as lurking house-trespass committed after sunset and before sunrise. This is the most aggravated form of trespass, combining stealth with nighttime commission. Punishment ranges from 3 to 14 years depending on purpose and whether hurt/death results.

question: What is house-breaking by night?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 446 IPC defines house-breaking by night.

Step 2: Establish combination - House-breaking committed after sunset and before sunrise.

Step 3: Define forcible nighttime entry - Breaking and entering at night.

Step 4: Note traditional burglary - Classic definition of burglary in common law.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Enhanced due to nighttime; up to 14 years if for theft (Section 460).
answer: Section 446 IPC defines house-breaking by night as house-breaking committed after sunset and before sunrise. This combines forcible or unauthorized entry with nighttime commission, making it more serious than daytime house-breaking. Punishment varies from 3 to 14 years depending on the criminal purpose.

question: What is breaking in order to commit house-trespass?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 447 IPC defines breaking for house-trespass.

Step 2: Establish preparatory breaking - Breaking wall, lock, or fastening to commit house-trespass.

Step 3: Define facilitation - Creating passage for unlawful entry.

Step 4: Note inchoate offense - Punishes preparation even if trespass not completed.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both; punishes breaking itself.
answer: Section 447 IPC provides that whoever breaks any wall, lock, fastening, or does any act which causes such breaking in order to commit house-trespass, is said to commit breaking. This is punishable with imprisonment of either description up to 2 years, or fine, or both, even if the house-trespass is not actually committed.

question: What is punishment for house-trespass?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 448 IPC punishes basic house-trespass.

Step 2: Establish unlawful entry - Criminal trespass in dwelling or property storage.

Step 3: Define privacy violation - Entering protected private spaces without authority.

Step 4: Note base offense - Other sections provide enhanced punishment based on manner, time, and purpose.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 1 year or fine up to Rs. 1000 or both.
answer: Section 448 IPC provides that whoever commits house-trespass shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 1 year, or with fine which may extend to Rs. 1000, or with both. This is basic house-trespass without aggravating circumstances.

question: What is house-trespass to commit offense punishable with death?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 449 IPC deals with house-trespass for capital offense.

Step 2: Establish extreme criminal purpose - Intent to commit offense punishable with death.

Step 3: Define gravest home invasion - Planning most serious crimes in dwelling.

Step 4: Note maximum danger to occupants - Intruder planning capital offense.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine; reflects extreme threat.
answer: Section 449 IPC provides that whoever commits house-trespass in order to the committing of any offense punishable with death, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term not exceeding 10 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is house-trespass for committing offense punishable with life imprisonment?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 450 IPC deals with house-trespass for serious offenses.

Step 2: Establish serious purpose - Intent to commit offense punishable with life imprisonment.

Step 3: Define grave threat - Planning very serious crime in dwelling.

Step 4: Note stepped punishment - Less than death penalty offenses but still grave.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine; reflects serious criminal intent.
answer: Section 450 IPC provides that whoever commits house-trespass in order to the committing of any offense punishable with imprisonment for life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is house-trespass to commit offense punishable with imprisonment?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 451 IPC deals with house-trespass for imprisonment offenses.

Step 2: Establish criminal purpose - Intent to commit offense punishable with imprisonment.

Step 3: Define lesser but still serious violation - Home invasion for criminal purpose.

Step 4: Note catch-all provision - Covers offenses not punishable with death or life.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 2 years and fine; graduated based on intended offense severity.
answer: Section 451 IPC provides that whoever commits house-trespass in order to the committing of any offense punishable with imprisonment, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, and shall also be liable to fine. This covers house-trespass committed to facilitate any offense punishable with imprisonment other than death or life.

question: What is house-trespass after preparation for hurt, assault or wrongful restraint?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 452 IPC deals with prepared house-trespass.

Step 2: Establish preparation for violence - Made preparation for causing hurt, assault, or wrongful restraint.

Step 3: Define armed or ready intruder - Shows planning and willingness to use force.

Step 4: Note dangerous preparation - More threatening than unprepared trespasser.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine; reflects preparation for violence.
answer: Section 452 IPC provides that whoever commits house-trespass, having made preparation for causing hurt to any person or for assaulting any person, or for wrongfully restraining any person, or for putting any person in fear of hurt, or of assault, or of wrongful restraint, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is punishment for lurking house-trespass or house-breaking?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 453 IPC punishes lurking house-trespass and house-breaking.

Step 2: Establish aggravated entry - Either concealed entry or forcible/unauthorized entry.

Step 3: Define enhanced culpability - Stealth or force makes trespass more serious.

Step 4: Note base level for aggravated trespass - Foundation for further enhanced punishments.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 2 years and fine; more than simple house-trespass (1 year).
answer: Section 453 IPC provides that whoever commits lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is lurking house-trespass or house-breaking to commit offense punishable with imprisonment?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 454 IPC deals with aggravated house-breaking for crime.

Step 2: Establish combination - Lurking/house-breaking plus intent to commit imprisonable offense.

Step 3: Define compound aggravation - Manner of entry plus criminal purpose.

Step 4: Note serious home invasion - Stealthy or forcible entry for criminal purpose.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine; reflects combined factors.
answer: Section 454 IPC provides that whoever commits lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, in order to the committing of any offense punishable with imprisonment, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the offense intended to be committed is theft, the term of imprisonment may be extended to 10 years.

question: What is lurking house-trespass or house-breaking after preparation for hurt, assault or wrongful restraint?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 455 IPC deals with armed/prepared aggravated house-breaking.

Step 2: Establish triple threat - Lurking/breaking + preparation for violence.

Step 3: Define maximum danger - Stealthy or forcible entry by person prepared for violence.

Step 4: Note extreme threat to occupants - Armed prepared intruder entering secretly or by force.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine; severe due to grave danger.
answer: Section 455 IPC provides that whoever commits lurking house-trespass, or house-breaking, having made preparation for causing hurt to any person, or for assaulting any person, or for wrongfully restraining any person, or for putting any person in fear of hurt or of assault or of wrongful restraint, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is lurking house-trespass or house-breaking by night?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 456 IPC deals with nighttime aggravated trespass.

Step 2: Establish time aggravation - Committed after sunset and before sunrise.

Step 3: Define nighttime danger - Occupants vulnerable, detection harder.

Step 4: Note traditional aggravating factor - Nighttime commission historically serious.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine; nighttime adds severity.
answer: Section 456 IPC provides that whoever commits lurking house-trespass by night, or house-breaking by night, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is lurking house-trespass or house-breaking by night to commit offense punishable with imprisonment?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 457 IPC deals with nighttime house-breaking for crime.

Step 2: Establish compound aggravation - Night + lurking/breaking + criminal purpose.

Step 3: Define serious threat - Nighttime stealthy or forcible entry for crime.

Step 4: Note multiple aggravating factors - Time, manner, and purpose all enhance culpability.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine; if theft intended: up to 14 years (Section 460).
answer: Section 457 IPC provides that whoever commits lurking house-trespass by night, or house-breaking by night, in order to the committing of any offense punishable with imprisonment, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 5 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is lurking house-trespass or house-breaking by night after preparation for hurt, assault or wrongful restraint?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 458 IPC deals with nighttime armed house-breaking.

Step 2: Establish maximum aggravation - Night + lurking/breaking + preparation for violence.

Step 3: Define gravest home invasion - Armed nighttime break-in.

Step 4: Note all aggravating factors present - Time, manner, and dangerous preparation.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 14 years and fine; reflects extreme danger.
answer: Section 458 IPC provides that whoever commits lurking house-trespass by night, or house-breaking by night, having made preparation for causing hurt to any person or for assaulting any person, or for wrongfully restraining any person, or for putting any person in fear of hurt, or of assault, or of wrongful restraint, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 14 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is grievous hurt caused while committing lurking house-trespass or house-breaking?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 459 IPC deals with violent house-breaking.

Step 2: Establish actual violence - Grievous hurt caused or attempt to cause death/grievous hurt.

Step 3: Define escalation to violence - Property crime becomes violent crime.

Step 4: Note serious harm to occupants - Home invasion resulting in serious injury.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment for life or up to 10 years and fine; reflects grave violence.
answer: Section 459 IPC provides that whoever, whilst committing lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, causes grievous hurt to any person or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt to any person, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is punishment when death or grievous hurt caused by members of group during lurking house-trespass by night?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 460 IPC establishes group liability for violent nighttime burglary.

Step 2: Establish joint enterprise - All members of group liable if any causes death/grievous hurt.

Step 3: Define collective responsibility - Group crime makes all liable for worst outcome.

Step 4: Note deterrent against group crimes - Discourages gang burglaries.

Step 5: Understand punishment - If grievous hurt: up to 14 years; if death: as for murder or culpable homicide.
answer: Section 460 IPC provides that if, at the time of committing lurking house-trespass by night or house-breaking by night, any person guilty of such offense shall voluntarily cause or attempt to cause death or grievous hurt to any person, every person jointly concerned in committing such offense shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 14 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is dishonestly breaking open receptacle containing property?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 461 IPC deals with breaking containers.

Step 2: Establish container violation - Breaking receptacle, box, safe, etc. containing property.

Step 3: Define dishonest purpose - Breaking with intent to commit theft or mischief.

Step 4: Note preparatory to theft - Breaking container facilitates theft of contents.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both; punishes breaking itself.
answer: Section 461 IPC provides that whoever dishonestly or with intent to commit mischief, breaks open or unfastens any closed receptacle which contains or which he believes to contain property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, or with fine, or with both.

question: What is breaking open receptacle when person entrusted with custody?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 462 IPC deals with breach of trust in breaking containers.

Step 2: Establish custodial relationship - Person entrusted with receptacle.

Step 3: Define abuse of trust - Using position to break open container.

Step 4: Note enhanced culpability - Betrayal of trust aggravates offense.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both; higher than Section 461 (2 years).
answer: Section 462 IPC provides that whoever, being entrusted with any closed receptacle which contains or which he believes to contain property, without having authority to open the same, dishonestly, or with intent to commit mischief, breaks open or unfastens that receptacle, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, or with fine, or with both.

question: What is forgery?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 463 IPC defines forgery.

Step 2: Establish false document creation - Making false document or electronic record intending it be believed genuine.

Step 3: Define fraudulent purpose - Intent to cause damage, support claim, cause property delivery, enter contract, or commit fraud.

Step 4: Note includes alterations - Altering genuine documents also forgery.

Step 5: Understand punishment (Section 465) - Basic forgery: up to 2 years; specific types: up to life imprisonment.
answer: Section 463 IPC defines forgery as whoever makes any false document or false electronic record or part of a document or electronic record, with intent to cause damage or injury, to the public or to any person, or to support any claim or title, or to cause any person to part with property, or to enter into any express or implied contract, or with intent to commit fraud or that fraud may be committed, commits forgery.

question: What is making a false document?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 464 IPC defines making false document.

Step 2: Establish various methods - Making, signing, sealing, altering document dishonestly/fraudulently.

Step 3: Define scope - Includes creating in another's name, altering genuine documents, using seal fraudulently.

Step 4: Note comprehensive definition - Covers all ways of creating false documents.

Step 5: Understand significance - Establishes what constitutes 'making' for forgery purposes.
answer: Section 464 IPC provides that a person makes a false document who dishonestly or fraudulently: (1) makes, signs, seals or executes a document intending it be believed made by someone else; (2) alters or tampers with a genuine document; (3) causes someone to sign, seal or execute document not knowing contents; (4) uses seal/plate/instrument to make impression intending it be believed made by different person.

question: What is punishment for forgery?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 465 IPC punishes forgery.

Step 2: Establish basic punishment - General forgery not otherwise specified.

Step 3: Define graduated system - Specific types have enhanced punishments.

Step 4: Note hierarchy - Court records (7 years), valuable security/will (life/10 years), cheating (7 years), reputation (3 years).

Step 5: Understand punishment - Basic: up to 2 years or fine or both.
answer: Section 465 IPC provides that whoever commits forgery shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, or with fine, or with both. Specific forgeries have enhanced punishments under Sections 466-469.

question: What is forgery of record of court or of public register?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 466 IPC deals with forgery of official records.

Step 2: Establish protected documents - Court records, public registers, official certificates.

Step 3: Define public trust violation - Tampering with documents relied upon by public.

Step 4: Note undermines justice - False court records pervert justice.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine; reflects importance of official records.
answer: Section 466 IPC provides that whoever forges a document or electronic record purporting to be a record or proceeding of or in a Court of Justice, or a register of birth, baptism, marriage or burial, or a register kept by a public servant as such, or a certificate or document purporting to be made by a public servant in his official capacity, or an authority to institute or defend a suit, or to take any proceedings therein, or to confess judgment, or a power of attorney, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is forgery of valuable security, will, or authority to adopt a son?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 467 IPC deals with forgery of critical documents.

Step 2: Establish crucial documents - Valuable securities, wills, authority to adopt, documents to transfer securities.

Step 3: Define grave impact - Can deprive heirs, transfer property fraudulently, affect family rights.

Step 4: Note maximum seriousness - Most important documents in property and family law.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment for life or up to 10 years and fine; highest forgery punishment.
answer: Section 467 IPC provides that whoever forges a document which purports to be a valuable security or a will, or an authority to adopt a son, or which purports to give authority to any person to make or transfer any valuable security, or to receive the principal, interest or dividends thereon, or to receive or deliver any money, movable property, or valuable security, or any document purporting to be an acquittance or receipt acknowledging the payment of money, or delivery of property or valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is forgery for purpose of cheating?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 468 IPC deals with forgery for fraud.

Step 2: Establish fraudulent intent - Forgery with intent document be used for cheating.

Step 3: Define combination offense - Forgery plus fraud purpose.

Step 4: Note commercial crime application - Common in financial frauds.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine; reflects fraudulent purpose.
answer: Section 468 IPC provides that whoever commits forgery, intending that the document or electronic record forged shall be used for the purpose of cheating, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is forgery for purpose of harming reputation?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 469 IPC deals with forgery to harm reputation.

Step 2: Establish malicious intent - Forgery intended to damage reputation.

Step 3: Define reputational harm - Creating false documents to defame.

Step 4: Note attack on intangible interest - Reputation is valuable though intangible.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine; reflects harm to reputation.
answer: Section 469 IPC provides that whoever commits forgery, intending that the document or electronic record forged shall harm the reputation of any party, or knowing that it is likely to be used for that purpose, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is forged document or electronic record?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 470 IPC defines forged document.

Step 2: Establish post-forgery status - Document becomes 'forged' once forgery completed.

Step 3: Define scope - Remains forged regardless of possession.

Step 4: Note legal classification - Determines applicability of using/possessing forged document offenses.

Step 5: Understand significance - Foundation for Sections 471-474 on using forged documents.
answer: Section 470 IPC provides that a false document or false electronic record made wholly or in part by forgery is designated a 'forged document' or 'forged electronic record'. This classification applies to documents/records created through the offense of forgery as defined in Section 463.

question: What is using as genuine a forged document or electronic record?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 471 IPC deals with using forged documents.

Step 2: Establish knowledge requirement - Knowing or having reason to believe document is forged.

Step 3: Define fraudulent use - Using forged document as if genuine.

Step 4: Note equal culpability - Using forged document as serious as making it.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Same as for forging that type of document; up to life for valuable security/will.
answer: Section 471 IPC provides that whoever fraudulently or dishonestly uses as genuine any document or electronic record which he knows or has reason to believe to be a forged document or forged record, shall be punished in the same manner as if he had forged such document or record. Punishment matches the forgery offense for that document type.

question: What is making or possessing counterfeit seal, etc., with intent to commit forgery punishable under Section 467?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 472 IPC deals with possessing forgery instruments.

Step 2: Establish preparatory offense - Making or possessing counterfeit seals, plates for forgery.

Step 3: Define intent requirement - For purpose of committing forgery.

Step 4: Note preventive approach - Punishes possession of tools before actual forgery.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment for life or up to 7 years and fine; reflects serious preparation.
answer: Section 472 IPC provides that whoever makes or counterfeits any seal, plate or other instrument for making an impression, intending that the same shall be used for the purpose of committing any forgery which would be punishable under Section 467 (valuable security, will, authority to adopt), or has in his possession any such seal, plate or instrument, knowing the same to be counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is making or possessing counterfeit seal, etc., with intent to commit forgery punishable otherwise?
reasoning:
Step 1: Locate provision - Section 473 IPC deals with possessing forgery tools for other forgeries.

Step 2: Establish lesser forgery intent - For forgeries other than valuable securities/wills.

Step 3: Define preparatory offense - Making/possessing instruments for less serious forgeries.

Step 4: Note still serious - Preparatory steps toward forgery.

Step 5: Understand punishment - Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine; same as Section 472 but for less serious forgeries.
answer: Section 473 IPC provides that whoever makes or counterfeits any seal, plate or other instrument for making an impression, intending that the same shall be used for the purpose of committing any forgery which would be punishable under any section of this Chapter other than Section 467, or has in possession any such seal, plate or instrument, knowing the same to be counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and shall also be liable to fine.

question: What is having possession of document described in Section 466 or 467, knowing it to be forged and intending to use it as genuine?
reasoning:
Step 1: Identify provision - Section 474 IPC deals with possessing forged official documents.

Step 2: Establish knowledge and intent - Knowing document forged, intending to use as genuine.
